postCopy.instagram: A dead man who never existed. A killer hiding behind a stolen name. 🔍 Can you unmask them before they vanish? Play Case #036 — The Man Who Wasn't There. Link in bio.
postCopy.tiktok: Free daily murder mystery game: solve a stolen identity case set in a 1932 grand hotel. New deduction puzzle every day — can you crack today's case? Play at thedailywhodunit.com

marketing.imagePrompt: A grand Central European hotel lobby in 1932, art deco architecture with ornate gilded columns, deep crimson carpets, and a sweeping marble staircase. A single leather travel trunk sits abandoned near the concierge desk beneath a vast chandelier. A shadow of a man is cast across the floor — but no figure is visible. Muted ochres, deep burgundy, and pale gold. Painterly, cinematic, slightly surreal. Style of Wes Anderson meets Otto Dix.
marketing.animationPrompt: Slow push into the grand hotel lobby from outside through the revolving brass door. The chandelier flickers once. A shadow moves across the crimson carpet toward the staircase — but no one is there. Papers flutter on the concierge desk. Hold on the abandoned trunk as the name tag slowly rotates to reveal a different name on the reverse. Fade to black.

rookie.title: The Man Who Wasn't There
rookie.flavor: A guest registered as Count Aldric Voss was found dead in Room 14. But the real Count Voss is alive and well in Vienna — he never checked in. Someone stole his identity to gain access to the hotel. The question is who, and why.
rookie.emptyState: The guest in Room 14 was never who he claimed to be. Neither is the killer.

items.name: Felix Morrow
items.detail: Travelling insurance broker, three days at the hotel.

items.name: Petra Szabo
items.detail: Hungarian antiques dealer staying in Room 9.

items.name: Dmitri Crane
items.detail: Freelance photographer with a room on the third floor.

items.name: Room 14
items.detail: The victim's locked guest room on the second floor.

items.name: Hotel Lobby
items.detail: The grand entrance hall watched by the night porter.

items.name: Linen Cupboard
items.detail: Storage corridor at the end of the second floor.

items.name: Poisoned Wine
items.detail: Crystal decanter found laced with potassium tartrate.

items.name: Silk Sash
items.detail: Decorative sash removed from the room's curtains.

items.name: Bronze Statuette
items.detail: Heavy decorative figure from the writing desk.

clues.heading: The Concierge Remembers
clues.text: The night porter recalls the guest in Room 14 arguing loudly with a man he recognized — Felix Morrow, a travelling insurance broker who had been staying at the hotel for three days prior.
clues.alone: Felix Morrow was seen confronting the victim the night before the body was discovered.

links.insight: Morrow's confrontation with the victim and the insurance policy naming him as sole beneficiary confirm he had both motive and prior grievance — this was not a chance killing.

clues.heading: A Half-Empty Decanter
clues.text: A crystal decanter of red wine on the writing desk was found half-empty. The hotel physician confirmed traces of potassium tartrate — a compound that in sufficient doses causes cardiac arrest — were dissolved in the remaining wine.
clues.alone: The victim was poisoned through wine left in his room.

links.insight: The poisoned wine and the forged passkey used to enter Room 14 confirm the killing was premeditated — the poison was placed during an unauthorized visit before the victim retired.

clues.heading: Letters in the Lining
clues.text: A hidden compartment in the victim's trunk contained correspondence addressed not to Count Voss but to a man named Eduard Halm — threatening letters demanding repayment of a debt or exposure of a financial crime. The letters were signed only with an initial: M.
clues.alone: The victim's true identity was Eduard Halm, a man being blackmailed — the stolen identity was his cover.

links.insight: The blackmail letters signed M and the seamstress hearing Morrow name Halm on the telephone together confirm Morrow was both the extortionist and the killer.

clues.heading: A Passkey Unaccounted For
clues.text: The hotel's master passkey log shows a key signed out under a staff alias on the evening of the death — the signature does not match any current employee. The key grants access to all guest rooms on the second floor, including Room 14.
clues.alone: Someone obtained unauthorized access to Room 14 using a forged staff credential.

links.insight: The forged passkey entry and Morrow's handwriting match on the log confirm he entered Room 14 undetected and had opportunity to introduce the poison into the decanter.

clues.heading: The Insurance File
clues.text: Felix Morrow's briefcase, examined by hotel security, contained a life insurance policy taken out on Eduard Halm — with Morrow listed as sole beneficiary. The policy was dated six weeks prior and worth a considerable sum.
clues.alone: Morrow stood to profit financially from Halm's death — this was not a crime of passion.

links.insight: Morrow's confrontation with the victim and the insurance policy naming him as sole beneficiary confirm he had both motive and prior grievance — this was not a chance killing.

clues.heading: Handwriting on the Log
clues.text: A handwriting expert compared the forged staff alias on the passkey log with Felix Morrow's hotel registration card. The letter formations in the K and the looped R are a clear match.
clues.alone: The forged passkey signature belongs to Felix Morrow.

links.insight: The poisoned wine and the forged passkey used to enter Room 14 confirm the killing was premeditated — the poison was placed during an unauthorized visit before the victim retired.

clues.heading: The Seamstress on the Third Floor
clues.text: A seamstress staying in Room 22 heard a man with a clipped northern accent on the telephone late at night, saying: 'Halm won't be a problem after tomorrow.' She identified the voice as coming from the room registered to Felix Morrow.
clues.alone: Morrow referred to the victim by his true name — Eduard Halm — proving he knew the stolen identity was a disguise.

links.insight: The blackmail letters signed M and the seamstress hearing Morrow name Halm on the telephone together confirm Morrow was both the extortionist and the killer.

clues.heading: Residue on the Decanter Stopper
clues.text: Forensic examination of the decanter stopper found a partial fingerprint in the wax seal — consistent with a male right hand. Felix Morrow's fingerprints, taken from his registration card, match the partial print.
clues.alone: Morrow's fingerprint was found on the poisoned decanter — placing him at the weapon.

links.insight: The forged passkey entry and Morrow's handwriting match on the log confirm he entered Room 14 undetected and had opportunity to introduce the poison into the decanter.

rookie.epilogue: Felix Morrow had been blackmailing Eduard Halm for months over a collapsed investment scheme. When Halm fled under a stolen identity, Morrow tracked him to the Grand Szarvas, forged a passkey, slipped poison into his wine decanter, and waited. The insurance policy was the final proof — Morrow had planned a murder dressed as a natural death. His fingerprint on the stopper and his handwriting on the passkey log sealed his fate. He was arrested at the Vienna train station the following morning.

detective.title: The Man Who Wasn't There
detective.flavor: A guest registered as Count Aldric Voss was found dead in Room 14. The real Count Voss never left Vienna. Someone stole his identity to slip inside the hotel undetected — and someone else followed them there to make sure they never left.
detective.emptyState: The guest register is a web of false names. One alias belongs to a killer.

clues.heading: The Concierge Remembers
clues.text: The night porter places Felix Morrow arguing with the guest in Room 14 at 9:15 pm. He also notes Petra Szabo — a Hungarian antiques dealer in Room 9 — lingering near Room 14 at 10:00 pm, though she did not knock or enter.
clues.alone: Both Morrow and Szabo had proximity to the victim that evening — only Morrow had a confrontation.

links.insight: Morrow's argument with the victim combined with the forged passkey log entry signed in his handwriting confirms he had both grievance and unauthorized access to Room 14.

clues.heading: The Decanter and the Dose
clues.text: The crystal decanter contained potassium tartrate — enough for cardiac arrest. The hotel physician noted the compound is used in certain photographic developing solutions. Dmitri Crane, a photographer in Room 7, had developing chemicals in his room.
clues.alone: The poison came from a photographic chemical — Crane had access to the compound but is not confirmed as the source.

links.insight: Crane possessed the chemical, but the insurance policy naming Morrow as sole beneficiary eliminates Crane as a financial motive suspect and redirects to Morrow as the one with cause to kill.

clues.heading: Letters and a Ledger
clues.text: The hidden trunk compartment held blackmail letters signed M and a personal ledger showing Eduard Halm — the victim's true name — had transferred funds to a shell account in Basel twice in the past year. The entries stop two months ago.
clues.alone: Halm stopped paying his blackmailer two months ago — the killing followed his defiance.

links.insight: Morrow's call to the Basel number and the ledger's Basel shell account confirm he was the blackmailer who escalated to murder when payments stopped.

clues.heading: The Passkey Log
clues.text: The master passkey log shows a key signed out under the alias 'K. Brenner — Maintenance' on the night of the death. Handwriting analysis of the signature compared with Felix Morrow's hotel registration card shows matching letter formations.
clues.alone: Morrow signed out a master passkey under a false name — placing him with access to Room 14.

links.insight: The forged passkey signature matching Morrow's handwriting and his fingerprint on the decanter stopper confirm he entered Room 14 and tampered with the wine.

clues.heading: The Insurance File
clues.text: Felix Morrow's briefcase contained a life insurance policy on Eduard Halm worth 40,000 marks, with Morrow as sole beneficiary. The policy was taken out six weeks ago. Ilse Vander — a lawyer in Room 18 — is listed as the witnessing solicitor.
clues.alone: Morrow arranged the insurance policy weeks before the killing — this was planned murder for financial gain.

links.insight: Morrow's argument with the victim combined with the forged passkey log entry signed in his handwriting confirms he had both grievance and unauthorized access to Room 14.

clues.heading: The Chemical Supply Room
clues.text: The hotel's photographic supply cabinet, used by guests for developing, shows one vial of potassium tartrate solution missing from inventory. The cabinet sign-out log has a page torn out — the page covering the evening of the death.
clues.alone: Someone removed the poison from the hotel's own supply room and destroyed the evidence of their access.

links.insight: Crane possessed the chemical, but the insurance policy naming Morrow as sole beneficiary eliminates Crane as a financial motive suspect and redirects to Morrow as the one with cause to kill.

clues.heading: The Late Night Call
clues.text: Hotel telephone records confirm a call placed from Room 11 — Felix Morrow's room — to an unregistered Basel number at 11:43 pm. A seamstress in Room 22 overheard Morrow say: 'Halm won't be a problem after tomorrow.'
clues.alone: Morrow called a Basel number and named Halm — hours before the body was found.

links.insight: Morrow's call to the Basel number and the ledger's Basel shell account confirm he was the blackmailer who escalated to murder when payments stopped.

clues.heading: Fingerprint on the Stopper
clues.text: A partial fingerprint lifted from the decanter stopper's wax seal matches Felix Morrow's right hand, taken from his hotel registration card. No other prints were found on the stopper's inner surface.
clues.alone: Morrow's fingerprint is on the poisoned decanter — placing his hand at the murder weapon.

links.insight: The forged passkey signature matching Morrow's handwriting and his fingerprint on the decanter stopper confirm he entered Room 14 and tampered with the wine.

detective.epilogue: Felix Morrow had tracked Eduard Halm across three countries. When Halm stopped paying, Morrow followed him to the Grand Szarvas under an insurance pretext, obtained the poison from the hotel's own supply room, forged a passkey, and introduced the compound into Halm's wine decanter. He left Crane's chemicals visible as a deliberate misdirect. The Basel phone call and his fingerprint on the stopper sealed it. He was taken off the Vienna Express at the border.

inspector.title: The Man Who Wasn't There
inspector.flavor: Count Aldric Voss never left Vienna. The man in Room 14 was Eduard Halm — a financier on the run from a blackmailer. Now Halm is dead, the hotel's guest register is a web of false names, and two guests share a secret that brought a killer directly to his target.
inspector.emptyState: Two people led the killer here. Only one pulled the trigger.

items.detail: Incapacitated the victim before the poison was introduced.

items.name: Provided the Chemical
items.detail: Supplied the potassium tartrate from hotel stocks.

items.name: Forged the Document
items.detail: Created the false passkey log entry.

items.name: Disposed of Evidence
items.detail: Removed or destroyed incriminating materials.

clues.heading: Three People, One Corridor
clues.text: The night porter places Felix Morrow arguing with the victim at 9:15 pm, Petra Szabo loitering near Room 14 at 10:00 pm, and an unrecognized man wearing a hotel porter's uniform entering Room 14 with a key at 11:20 pm. The false porter was Morrow's height and build.
clues.alone: Three individuals had proximity to Room 14 that evening — the false porter is the one who entered.

links.insight: The false porter seen entering Room 14 at 11:20 pm and the M-monogrammed cufflink found in the discarded uniform confirm Morrow impersonated hotel staff to gain undetected entry.

clues.heading: Two Compounds, One Victim
clues.text: The hotel physician's full toxicology report reveals two compounds in the victim's blood: potassium tartrate from the wine, and chloral hydrate — a sedative that would have rendered him incapacitated before the cardiac poison took effect. The chloral hydrate was not in the decanter.
clues.alone: The victim was first sedated, then poisoned — two separate compounds suggesting two people or two delivery methods.

links.insight: The missing chloral hydrate dose from Vander's medicine bag and her 10:45 pm appointment diary entry for Room 14 confirm she administered the sedative, acting as Morrow's accomplice.

clues.heading: The Ledger and the Alias
clues.text: Halm's personal ledger shows Basel payments, but a second set of entries — in different handwriting — records payments received from a 'Count A. Voss' for the provision of forged travel documents. The handwriting matches Petra Szabo's hotel registration card.
clues.alone: Petra Szabo forged the travel papers that allowed Halm to assume Count Voss's identity — she was part of the scheme from the beginning.

links.insight: Szabo's ledger entries confirming she sold the forged identity, and her earlier Basel call matching Morrow's Basel contact, reveal she inadvertently led the killer to his target.

clues.heading: The Uniform in the Cupboard
clues.text: A hotel porter's uniform — one size too large — was found bundled in the linen cupboard at the end of the second-floor corridor. A monogrammed cufflink caught in the sleeve bore the letter M, consistent with German manufacture and Felix Morrow's known monogram.
clues.alone: The killer wore a stolen porter's uniform and left a monogrammed cufflink — the initial M points to Felix Morrow.

links.insight: The false porter seen entering Room 14 at 11:20 pm and the M-monogrammed cufflink found in the discarded uniform confirm Morrow impersonated hotel staff to gain undetected entry.

clues.heading: The Lawyer's Medicine Bag
clues.text: Ilse Vander's room contained a legal medicine bag with a vial of chloral hydrate solution — one measured dose missing. Her appointment diary shows a notation for Room 14 at 10:45 pm, listed as 'document witnessing — Halm.'
clues.alone: Vander visited Halm at 10:45 pm under a professional pretext — the missing sedative dose matches the compound found in the victim's blood.

links.insight: The missing chloral hydrate dose from Vander's medicine bag and her 10:45 pm appointment diary entry for Room 14 confirm she administered the sedative, acting as Morrow's accomplice.

clues.heading: Two Calls to Basel
clues.text: Hotel telephone records show two calls the night of the death: a call from Room 9 — Petra Szabo's room — to an unregistered Basel number at 8:55 pm lasting four minutes, and a call from Room 11 — Felix Morrow's room — to the same Basel number at 11:43 pm lasting forty seconds.
clues.alone: Szabo and Morrow both called the same Basel number on the night of the killing — they were connected through a shared contact.

links.insight: Szabo's ledger entries confirming she sold the forged identity, and her earlier Basel call matching Morrow's Basel contact, reveal she inadvertently led the killer to his target.

clues.heading: Fingerprint on the Stopper
clues.text: A partial fingerprint lifted from the decanter stopper's wax seal matches Felix Morrow's right hand. The print was deposited after the seal was last broken — meaning Morrow handled the stopper during or after the poison was introduced.
clues.alone: Morrow's fingerprint on the stopper places his hand at the murder weapon after it was tampered with.

links.insight: Morrow's fingerprint on the decanter and the torn supply room log page confirm he sourced the poison from the hotel's own stocks and introduced it into the wine that night.

clues.heading: The Torn Supply Log
clues.text: The hotel's photographic chemical supply cabinet is missing one vial of potassium tartrate. The cabinet sign-out log has a page torn out covering the evening of the death. The torn stub shows indentations from a heavy pen — consistent with the nib of Morrow's fountain pen, found in his room.
clues.alone: Someone removed the poison from the hotel's own supply and destroyed the access record — the pen indentations point to Morrow.

links.insight: Morrow's fingerprint on the decanter and the torn supply room log page confirm he sourced the poison from the hotel's own stocks and introduced it into the wine that night.

inspector.epilogue: Ilse Vander sedated Halm at 10:45 pm under the cover of document witnessing. Morrow, wearing a stolen porter's uniform, entered at 11:20 pm and introduced the potassium tartrate — sourced from the hotel's own supply room — into the wine decanter. Petra Szabo had unwittingly set the trap: she forged Halm's identity papers and then called Basel to confirm his location, not knowing Morrow monitored the same network. Vander turned witness for the prosecution. Morrow was convicted in Prague in 1933. Szabo fled to Bucharest and was never formally charged.
